points.text: Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in each WebSocket message per client or channel.
Client stores last processed seq; discard any incoming message with seq <= stored value.
If messages arrive out of order, buffer them until missing seq arrives or request a resend.
Attach a unique message ID (e.g., UUID) and keep recent IDs in a short‑TTL cache for deduplication.
Make server-side processing idempotent by checking the message ID before applying business logic.
Secure the channel with TLS (wss://) and authenticate using short‑lived signed JWTs.
Include a nonce (jti) claim in JWTs and track used nonces to prevent token replay attacks.
Rotate tokens on reconnect and enforce re‑authentication for new WebSocket connections.
Optionally sign each message with an HMAC using a shared secret to verify integrity.
Implement exponential backoff for client reconnection attempts to avoid thundering herd.
Include a monotonically increasing sequence number in each WebSocket message payload.
Client stores the highest sequence number received and discards any message with a lower or equal number.
On reconnect, client sends its last received sequence; server resumes sending from the next number.
Use TLS (wss://) to encrypt the channel and protect tokens from interception.
Issue short‑lived JWTs with a unique jti claim and timestamp; server rejects reused jti values.
Include a nonce in the authentication token and validate its freshness on each connection.
Maintain a short‑term server cache of recent sequence numbers or token IDs to detect duplicates.
Enable WebSocket ping/pong keep‑alive frames to detect dead connections promptly.
If ordering is critical, route events through an ordered backend queue (e.g., Kafka) before sending.
Use TLS (wss://) for all WebSocket traffic to protect confidentiality and integrity.
Authenticate each connection with a short‑lived signed token (e.g., JWT) bound to client ID, verify on upgrade.
Include a per‑message nonce or monotonically increasing sequence number signed with HMAC; reject duplicates or out‑of‑order beyond allowed window.
Maintain server‑side last‑seen sequence per client and drop any message with sequence ≤ last seen to prevent replay.
Embed a timestamp in each message and reject those older than a few seconds, limiting replay time‑window.
Rotate tokens frequently, require re‑auth on reconnection, and provide immediate revocation for compromised tokens.
Assign a monotonically increasing sequence number per client session and discard messages with old or duplicate numbers.
Include a unique message ID and keep a short‑TTL cache of recent IDs to filter duplicate events.
Sign each WebSocket payload with an HMAC using a per‑connection secret; verify on receipt to ensure integrity.
Use short‑lived, connection‑bound tokens (e.g., JWT or opaque token) and rotate them on each reconnect.
Maintain a replay‑cache keyed by token + nonce and reject any payload that repeats within the cache window.
